- Actually, `[1 : n]` is `List.range (n - 1)` but starting at `1`. Wait, no: in Lean4, `[a : b]` is `List.range (b - a)` and then `+a`. So `[1 : 2] = [1]`, `[1 : 3] = [1, 2]`, etc. So for `n = 2`, `i` is `1`.
   - For `i = 1`, `j` in `[0 : i] = [0 : 1] = [0]`:
      - Check if `nums[0]! = 3 < nums[1]! = 1`? No. So no change to `dp`.
   - So `dp` remains `[1, 1]`, `maxInArray` is `1`.
   - The actual LIS is `1` (`[3]` or `[1]`).
   - `allSubseq` is `[[], [3], [1], [1, 3]]`, reversed: `[[], [3], [1], [3, 1]]`.
      - Increasing: `[[], [3], [1]]`.
      - `increasingSubseqLens = [0, 1, 1]`.
   - So `contains 1 [0, 1, 1]` (true), and all `≤ 1` (true).

It seems that for `nums = [3, 1]`, it works!

What about `nums = [1, 3]`:
   - `n = 2`.
   - `i = 1`.
   - `j = 0`.
      - Check if `nums[0]! = 1 < nums[1]! = 3`? Yes. And `dp[0]! + 1 = 2 > dp[1]! = 1`. So set `dp[1] = 2`.
   - `dp = [1, 2]`, max is `2`.
   - The actual LIS is `2` (`[1, 3]`).
   - `allSubseq` is `[[], [1], [3, 1], [3, 1, 3]]`, reversed: `[[], [1], [1, 3], [3, 1, 3]]`.
      - Increasing: `[[], [1], [1, 3]]`.
      - `increasingSubseqLens = [0, 1, 2]`.
   - `contains 2 [0, 1, 2]` (true), all `≤ 2` (true).

It seems to work again!

But earlier for `nums = [3, 1, 2]`:
   - `n = 3`, `i` in `[1, 2]`.
      - For `i = 1`, `j = 0`: `nums[0]! = 3 < nums[1]! = 1`? No.
      - For `i = 2`, `j` in `[0, 1]`:
         - `j = 0`: `nums[0]! = 3 < nums[2]! = 2`? No.
         - `j = 1`: `nums[1]! = 1 < nums[2]! = 2`? Yes. And `dp[1]! + 1 = 1 + 1 = 2 > dp[2]! = 1`. So set `dp[2] = 2`.
   - So `dp = [1, 1, 2]`, max is `2`.
   - Actual LIS is `2` (`[1, 2]` or `[3]`).
   - `allSubseq`: `[[], [3], [1], [1, 3], [2], [2, 3], [2, 1], [2, 1, 3]]`, reversed: `[[], [3], [1], [3, 1], [2], [3, 2], [1, 2], [3, 1, 2]]`.
      - Increasing: `[[], [3], [1], [2], [1, 2]]`.
      - `increasingSubseqLens = [0, 1, 1, 1, 2]`.
   - `contains 2 [0, 1, 1, 1, 2]` (true), all `≤ 2
